mnemonics.title: Dijkstra's Algorithm
mnemonics.content: Dijkstra: 'Does Queen Inspect Routes In Terrible Alphabet' - D(0)=0, Q=extract min, I=initialize, R=relaxation, T=traverse, A=all vertices - Start at source, always pick closest unvisited vertex, relax edges to find shortest paths

mnemonics.title: Bellman-Ford Algorithm
mnemonics.content: Bellman-Ford: 'Bells Fold Repeatedly, Every Edge Reaches New Distance' - B=initialize, F=for V-1 times, E=every edge, R=relax, N=negative cycle check - Can handle negative weights unlike Dijkstra

mnemonics.title: Prim's Algorithm
mnemonics.content: Prim's: 'Pick Repeatedly, Inexpensive Minimum Vertex' - Start from any vertex, repeatedly pick minimum weight edge connecting a vertex in tree to vertex outside, grows MST like a tree branch spreading

mnemonics.title: Kruskal's Algorithm
mnemonics.content: Kruskal's: 'Keep Recognizing Smallest Urgent Knots' - Sort all edges by weight, pick smallest edge that doesn't form cycle (use Union-Find), builds forest that merges into one tree

mnemonics.title: Shortest Path vs MST
mnemonics.content: Shortest Path = 'Door to Door Delivery' (one source to one destination); MST = 'Road Network Connecting All Cities' (all vertices connected with minimum cost)

mnemonics.title: Floyd-Warshall
mnemonics.content: Floyd-Warshall: 'Fast Way Over Distance Paths Using All Intermediate Nodes' - Uses dynamic programming, checks all intermediate vertices, finds all-pairs shortest paths, works with negative weights but not negative cycles

acronyms.usage: Use when you need to connect all vertices with minimum total edge weight, like designing network/road infrastructure
Use for finding shortest path in unweighted graphs - explores level by level from source
Use when finding shortest paths from one source vertex to all other vertices
Use when finding shortest paths between every pair of vertices (Floyd-Warshall)
Use in Kruskal's algorithm to detect cycles - tracks which vertices belong to same component

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a ripple/stone dropping in water at the source vertex - the ripples spread outward in concentric circles, each circle represents vertices at increasing distances. Always process the innermost ripple first.
Picture a tree growing in a forest - starting from one seed (vertex), branches (edges) grow outward to connect nearest unconnected trees, gradually forming one connected tree
Imagine sorting coins (edges) by weight and placing them one by one on a table, but you skip any coin that would create a closed loop - like building a bridge network where you never create circles
Think of a relay race - information (distance) gets passed along edges, each pass (iteration) potentially improving the time (distance), can propagate improvements V-1 times
A spanning tree is like a skeleton - it connects all bones (vertices) with minimum necessary connections (V-1 edges), no extra bones, no missing bones, forms a tree structure
If after V-1 relaxations, any edge can still be relaxed, it means you can keep going around a cycle and keep decreasing distance infinitely - like a perpetual money-making machine

analogies.analogy: Using GPS navigation - finding the quickest route from your home (source) to a specific destination, considering traffic/road distances
Laying telephone cables to connect all houses in a city with minimum total cable length - you don't care about routes, just total connectivity cost
Like collecting stamps - you always pick the nearest stamp (vertex with minimum distance) to add to your collection, assuming once picked it's the shortest
Like building a railway network by always laying the cheapest track segment available, as long as it doesn't create a circular route
When building a tree structure, adding an edge that creates a cycle is like trying to add an already-connected city to your road network - it's redundant
Like updating your knowledge - if you find a shorter route to somewhere through a friend, you update your stored best route, otherwise you keep your current best
